content:
# Hebrews Chapter 6 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/HEB_004_page_002.jpg, HEB_006_page_002.jpg

1. Therefore, leaving the word of the beginning of Christ, let us press on to maturity, not laying again a foundation of repentance from dead works and of faith toward God,

2. of instruction of baptisms and of laying on of hands, and of resurrection of [the] dead and of eternal judgment.

3. And this we will do, if indeed God permits.

4. For [it is] impossible for those who were once enlightened and tasted of the heavenly gift and became partakers of [the] Holy Spirit

5. and tasted [the] good word of God and [the] powers of [the] coming age,

6. and [then] fell away, to renew [them] again to repentance, since they are crucifying again for themselves the Son of God and putting [him] to open shame.

7. For earth which has drunk the rain coming upon it many times and produces vegetation useful for those on account of whom it is also cultivated, receives blessing from God;

8. but [that] bearing thorns and thistles [is] worthless and near to a curse, whose end [is] for burning.

9. But we are persuaded concerning you, beloved, of the better things and things belonging to salvation, even though we speak thus.

10. For God [is] not unjust to forget your work and the love which you showed toward his name, having ministered to the saints and [still] ministering.

11. But we desire each of you to show the same diligence toward the full assurance of the hope until [the] end,

12. that you not become sluggish, but imitators of those who through faith and patience inherit the promises.

13. For when God made promise to Abraham, since he had no one greater by whom to swear, he swore by himself,

14. saying, "Surely blessing I will bless you and multiplying I will multiply you."

15. And so, having patiently endured, he obtained the promise.

16. For men swear by the greater, and an oath for confirmation [is] to them an end of all dispute.

17. In which [matter], God, wanting more abundantly to show to the heirs of the promise the unchangeableness of his purpose, guaranteed [it] with an oath,

18. that through two unchangeable things, in which [it is] impossible [for] God to lie, we might have strong encouragement, we who fled for refuge to lay hold of the hope set before [us];

19. which we have as an anchor of the soul, both sure and firm and entering into the inner [place] behind the veil,

20. where Yeshua entered as a forerunner on our behalf, having become a high priest forever according to the order of Melchizedek.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **vv.4-6 — The impossible passage:** The five participial descriptions (enlightened, tasted, became partakers, tasted, fell away) describe genuine spiritual experience — this is not about those who merely heard. The impossibility is of renewal "again to repentance" — not of forgiveness per se, but of re-starting the process.

2. **v.14 — Hebraic infinitive absolute:** "Blessing I will bless... multiplying I will multiply" — the LXX preserves the Hebrew emphatic construction (infinitive absolute + finite verb). The doubling intensifies: God will certainly bless, certainly multiply.

3. **v.19 — "Anchor of the soul":** ankuran tēs psychēs — the only NT use of "anchor" as metaphor. The anchor goes "behind the veil" — an impossible physical image that makes perfect theological sense: our hope is anchored in the heavenly holy of holies.

— EMET, AI Translator
